title: Handling Out-of-Vocabulary Words
concept: If FastText meets an unseen word, it averages the vectors of its constituent subwords to make an educated semantic guess.

exercises.prompt: What does 'Out-of-Vocabulary' (OOV) mean in machine learning?

options.text: A word that was not present in the training data of the AI model.
A word that is misspelled in a dictionary.
A word that has more than ten letters.

exercises.explain: An Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) word is simply any word that was not present in the dataset the AI used during its training phase.

cards.title: Meeting New Words
cards.body: What happens when an AI model meets a word it has never seen before? Older systems simply give up and treat it as a complete mystery. Let's learn how FastText solves this puzzle by using its clever subword superpowers!

cards.title: What is OOV?
cards.body: Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) words are words that your AI model has never seen during its training. While older models get completely stuck on them, FastText solves this by breaking the unknown word into smaller chunks (subwords) and averaging their vectors to guess the meaning.

cards.title: The Hybrid Fruit Guessing Game
cards.body: Imagine you encounter a brand-new hybrid fruit called a 'straw-grape-apple'. Even if you have never seen one before, your brain instantly understands what it might taste like by breaking the name down into 'straw', 'grape', and 'apple' and blending their flavors. FastText does the exact same thing with word parts!

cards.title: FastText in Action
cards.body: If FastText encounters the unseen word 'sub-zero', it breaks it down into pieces it already knows, like '<su', 'sub', 'ub-', '-ze', 'zer', 'ero>'. By averaging the vectors of these pieces, it successfully guesses that the word relates to cold temperatures.

cards.title: The Dictionary Myth
cards.body: Many believe that if a word isn't in the official dictionary, an AI can never understand it. FastText busts this myth! Because it looks at prefixes, suffixes, and word roots, it can handle typos, slang, and brand-new words with ease.

cards.title: Comparison: Unknown Words
cards.body: Traditional models fail when they see a new word because they view words as solid blocks. FastText views words as Lego structures that can be understood by their individual bricks.

right.text: Sees 'unfriend' -> Splits into 'un' + 'friend' -> Returns smart average vector.

exercises.prompt: How do traditional word models (that don't use subwords) handle brand-new, unseen words?

options.text: They ask the user to type in a definition.
They usually replace them with a generic 'unknown' placeholder, losing all meaning.
They automatically search the internet for a dictionary.
They crash and stop running immediately.

exercises.explain: Older models treat all unseen words as a single 'unknown' token, losing all specific meaning. FastText uses subwords to build a unique, meaningful guess.

exercises.prompt: True or False: FastText can guess the meaning of a word it has never seen before.
exercises.explain: FastText can easily handle brand-new words by breaking them down into smaller pieces (subwords) that it has already learned during training.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence to describe how FastText calculates the vector for an unknown word.
exercises.sentence: When FastText meets a new word, it breaks it into subwords and [blank] their vectors.
exercises.explain: FastText takes all the subwords of the new word and averages their vectors to create a representation for the unseen word.

exercises.prompt: Identify which AI model is using FastText's subword approach to handle the unseen word 'unplayable':

options.text: Model A replaces 'unplayable' with a generic '[UNK]' token because it has never seen the full word before.
Model B splits 'unplayable' into 'un', 'play', and 'able' and combines their vectors to represent the word.
Model C ignores the word 'unplayable' completely and deletes it from the sentence.

exercises.explain: Option B is correct because FastText breaks the word into subwords like 'un', 'play', and 'able' to construct a vector, whereas Option A suggests a simple generic placeholder.

exercises.prompt: Your AI system just encountered the brand-new word 'superfasttext' which is not in its vocabulary. How will FastText handle this without failing?

options.text: It flags the word as an error and halts the entire program.
It randomly assigns a vector from a dictionary of known words.
It breaks the word into subwords (like 'super', 'fast', 'text') and averages their vectors.
It ignores the word entirely and skips to the next one.

exercises.explain: By splitting the unknown word 'superfasttext' into subwords like 'super', 'fast', and 'text', FastText can combine their known vectors to estimate a very accurate meaning for the whole new word!
